item.title: Australian tourist pleads guilty in fatal crash that killed Fijian in Auckland
item.description: An Australian man has pleaded guilty to reduced charges over the fatal crash that claimed the life of Fijian Arvind “Ronald” Kumar near Auckland Airport last year. According to the New Zealand Herald , 37-year-old Melbourne resident Michael Rockafella admitted guilt to lesser charges after originally being charged with manslaughter.
item.content:encoded:
An Australian man has pleaded guilty to reduced charges over the fatal crash that claimed the life of Fijian Arvind “Ronald” Kumar near Auckland Airport last year.

According to the New Zealand Herald , 37-year-old Melbourne resident Michael Rockafella admitted guilt to lesser charges after originally being charged with manslaughter.

Mr Kumar, 45, was driving his wife to Auckland Airport at about 4am on July 30 last year so she could travel to Fiji to attend her aunt’s funeral when the crash occurred.

Court documents released publicly for the first time reveal Rockafella had spent the early hours of the morning at SkyCity Casino before driving a rental ute towards his airport hotel at high speed.

Authorities say he was about eight times over the high-risk threshold for methamphetamine intoxication, almost twice the legal alcohol limit, and also had traces of cannabis and a sedative in his system.

Police said Rockafella initially told investigators he had consumed only two whiskey and cola drinks.

CCTV footage showed he had already run a red light shortly after arriving in Auckland. After leaving the casino at 3.39am, his driving became increasingly dangerous.

The New Zealand Herald reports he was clocked travelling at 142km/h in a 100km/h zone on State Highway 20 and was seen weaving aggressively through traffic, including forcing his vehicle into a narrow gap between a moving car and a light truck.

When traffic was diverted from a closed section of the motorway, Rockafella allegedly continued driving erratically, nearly colliding with another vehicle and travelling in a bicycle lane before the fatal crash.

Read Today’s Fiji Times Read Todays Fiji Times Today's Paper Subscribe Trending Stories Grower urges military deployment to save crushing season Investigation nears end $650m debt write-off defended The walk for hope Former inmates urged to return to villages © 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Twelve workers lose jobs as court orders closure of South Seas Club
item.description: Twelve employees of Lautoka’s South Seas Club are jobless after the popular venue was abruptly closed under a court order, leaving workers locked out and uncertain about their jobs. The court order, executed on Monday, handed management of the club to an interim committee. When staff reported for work the following morning, they found the gates locked and a notice stating the club was “under the a
item.content:encoded:
Twelve employees of Lautoka’s South Seas Club are jobless after the popular venue was abruptly closed under a court order, leaving workers locked out and uncertain about their jobs.

The court order, executed on Monday, handed management of the club to an interim committee. When staff reported for work the following morning, they found the gates locked and a notice stating the club was “under the administrators of South Seas Club” and temporarily closed until further notice.

Employee Adi Laisa Volatabu said staff had received no communication from their employer and did not know where to turn for help.

Sera Tukana said workers were denied entry and instructed to surrender all club keys.

“The least they could do was give us notice and time to look for work somewhere else,” she said.

The closure has also affected tenants. Restaurant owner Hafiz Rahman said he was forced to shut a business he had operated at the club for more than 20 years.

“It came as a real shock and as a tenant I wished that I could have been told before this was going to happen,” he said.

Staff member Salote Ratu said three customers had already paid to hire the club’s hall, including one who had paid $1000 in full.

Former manager Jan Mohammed who’s in Japan said he would make an official statement after consulting with his lawyer.

The South Seas Club was ordered to be handed over to an interim committee in 2022, but the transfer was delayed while an appeal was before the Court of Appeal. Last week, the appeal was dismissed, allowing the committee led by Sanjay Prasad, Cecil James and Raymond Singh to assume control.

Lawyer Anil Prasad said his clients were carrying out the court’s orders. Asked about the workers, he said the court order had to be executed.

The affected employees have since approached the Ministry of Employment for advice on their rights and entitlements.

Employment Minister Agni Deo Singh said labour officers at the Lautoka officer were looking into the matter.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Government commits to dialogue over Momi Bay land dispute
item.description: The Coalition Government has pledged to pursue dialogue to resolve the long-running Momi Bay land issue, saying it is committed to balancing the concerns of traditional landowners with national development interests. In a statement, Minister for Lands Filimoni Vosarogo acknowledged the concerns raised by traditional landowners over the 2005 land swap associated with the Momi Bay Marriott Hotel dev
item.content:encoded:
The Coalition Government has pledged to pursue dialogue to resolve the long-running Momi Bay land issue, saying it is committed to balancing the concerns of traditional landowners with national development interests.

In a statement, Minister for Lands Filimoni Vosarogo acknowledged the concerns raised by traditional landowners over the 2005 land swap associated with the Momi Bay Marriott Hotel development.

He said the Government inherited a complex issue and was committed to addressing it “responsibly and in good faith.”

“Our Coalition Government has inherited this complex issue and is determined to approach it with fairness, transparency, and respect for all parties involved,” Mr Vosarogo said.

The Minister noted that while the land holds deep cultural and traditional significance for landowners, the development also carries significant commercial implications because of the Fiji National Provident Fund’s investment and its contribution to the country’s tourism industry.

He said the 2013 Constitution had created a legal framework that prevented simply reversing the land swap, making dialogue the most practical path forward.

“As a government, we are committed to finding the right balance. We will not ignore the legitimate concerns of our traditional landowners, nor will we undermine important commercial investments that benefit all Fijians, including through the FNPF,” Mr Vosarogo said.

The Minister called on all key stakeholders to work together toward an amicable resolution.

“We urge all parties — the traditional landowners, the iTaukei Land Trust Board (TLTB), and the FNPF — to engage in constructive and inclusive dialogue. We will facilitate good-faith discussions aimed at an amicable resolution that honors customary rights, addresses historical grievances where possible, and ensures the sustainable development of Momi Bay for the benefit of current and future generations.”

Mr Vosarogo said the Government believes open communication and mutual respect are essential to resolving the longstanding dispute and confirmed that the Ministry of Lands will coordinate discussions with all relevant stakeholders.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Shore Buses condemns stone-throwing incident
item.description: Shore Buses has condemned a stone-throwing incident involving one of its buses near the Nadonumai Roundabout, warning that such behaviour could have resulted in serious injury or loss of life. In a statement posted on social media, the company said the incident occurred at around 7:00pm on Thursday, when a group of young boys allegedly threw stones at a bus travelling through the area.
item.content:encoded:
Shore Buses has condemned a stone-throwing incident involving one of its buses near the Nadonumai Roundabout, warning that such behaviour could have resulted in serious injury or loss of life.

In a statement posted on social media, the company said the incident occurred at around 7:00pm on Thursday, when a group of young boys allegedly threw stones at a bus travelling through the area.

The company said the stones narrowly missed the driver and, fortunately, no passengers or staff were injured.

“By God’s grace, no passengers or our bus driver were injured,” the statement said.

Shore Buses believes the attacks may be linked to some youths becoming upset after being refused travel because they did not pay the required fare.

“This type of behaviour is believed to be happening because some youths become upset when they are not allowed to board the bus without paying their bus fare.”

The company stressed that all passengers are required to pay the fare, noting that the adult fare from Nadonumai to Suva is $1.25.

“Let us be clear: everyone must pay the required fare to travel.”

Shore Buses appealed directly to young people in the area to stop targeting public transport.

“To the young youths of Nadonumai, please STOP stoning buses. This is extremely dangerous and could seriously injure or even kill someone.”

The company also urged those responsible to consider the potential consequences of their actions.

“What if your mother, father, grandparents, brothers, sisters or friends were inside that bus and those stones hit them? Would you want that to happen?”

Shore Buses has called on parents, guardians, community leaders and residents to speak with young people about the dangers of such behaviour, saying attacks on buses place innocent lives at risk and damage public property.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Chaudhry rejects call to close Viti Levu sugar mills
item.description: National Farmers Union general secretary Mahendra Chaudhry has dismissed calls by People’s Alliance Party president Ratu Joe Dimuri to close sugar mills on Viti Levu and shift the industry to Vanua Levu, describing the proposal as politically insensitive and harmful to the country’s sugar sector. Mr Chaudhry said Fiji’s struggling sugar industry needed rational decision-making and an inclusive dia
item.content:encoded:
National Farmers Union general secretary Mahendra Chaudhry has dismissed calls by People’s Alliance Party president Ratu Joe Dimuri to close sugar mills on Viti Levu and shift the industry to Vanua Levu, describing the proposal as politically insensitive and harmful to the country’s sugar sector.

Mr Chaudhry said Fiji’s struggling sugar industry needed rational decision-making and an inclusive dialogue involving all stakeholders, rather than what he described as “politically insensitive comments.”

“It is time for some rational decision making regarding the sugar industry and this can only be achieved through an all-inclusive stakeholders dialogue. Not through politically insensitive comments,” he said.

He argued that cane growers, who hold a 70 per cent stake in the industry, had been sidelined since the Bainimarama government dismantled key industry institutions in 2009.

“Since then, the industry has been run by the Sugar Ministry and FSC.”

Mr Chaudhry also criticised the Coalition Government for failing to fulfil its promise to hold Sugar Cane Growers Council elections and questioned the effectiveness of the bipartisan Parliamentary Select Committee on Sugar.

“Not a single meeting of the committee was held until 3 July this year, and only after the National Farmers Union wrote to the Prime Minister advising him to summon the committee to intervene in the 2026 harvest dispute over cane prices.”

He said the Government could not expect the industry to recover without listening to growers and taking meaningful action.

“Government cannot expect an ailing industry to right itself unless it is prepared to listen and take action.”

Responding directly to Mr Dimuri’s proposal, Mr Chaudhry described the suggestion as “irrational and irresponsible” and warned it would have serious consequences for the country’s economy.

“Such reckless and regionally biased comments from its President Joe Dimuri must be denounced.”

He questioned whether Mr Dimuri had considered the impact on the approximately 10,000 active cane farmers, around 75 per cent of whom are based in the Western Division.

“Has he considered the devastating socio-economic consequences of such a move on the 150,000 people directly or indirectly dependent on the industry for their livelihood? And on Fiji’s rural economy?”

Mr Chaudhry also criticised the lack of progress on agricultural diversification, saying it had become little more than a political slogan.

“There is no real plan, no markets, no infrastructure, and no serious investment to turn those lands into productive alternatives. Empty words will not feed the more than 200,000 people who still rely on the sugar industry.”

He maintained that cane growers were not responsible for the industry’s decline.

“Cane growers are not the problem. The miller is. Growers continue to cultivate cane even though it is no longer financially viable for them to do so.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fiji Council of Churches urges responsible Christian ministry in schools
item.description: The Fiji Council of Churches (FCC) is calling for Christian ministry in schools to be conducted responsibly and in line with Ministry of Education policies following public concern over a recent religious session held at a secondary school. In a statement, FCC General Secretary Reverend Sepiuta Hala’api’api said schools must remain places of learning, safety and inclusion, where any religious acti
item.content:encoded:
The Fiji Council of Churches (FCC) is calling for Christian ministry in schools to be conducted responsibly and in line with Ministry of Education policies following public concern over a recent religious session held at a secondary school.

In a statement, FCC General Secretary Reverend Sepiuta Hala’api’api said schools must remain places of learning, safety and inclusion, where any religious activity respects the wellbeing, dignity and rights of every student.

“The Fiji Council of Churches affirms that Christian ministry within schools must always be carried out in accordance with the policies and approval processes of the Ministry of Education,” Reverend Hala’api’api said.

He noted that Fiji has long-established programmes for Christian Fellowship, school devotions and Religious Education, delivered by trained and authorised organisations such as Scripture Union and recognised church partners.

“These ministries have served schools for many years by providing age-appropriate, respectful and accountable Christian engagement in partnership with school leadership and the Ministry of Education.”

While reaffirming that proclaiming the Gospel remains central to the Church’s mission, Reverend Hala’api’api stressed that how it is shared is equally important.

“The example of Jesus Christ calls Christians to share the Good News with humility, compassion, wisdom and respect. Ministry among children and young people requires pastoral sensitivity, safeguarding awareness, and an understanding of the cultural, social, and educational context in which they live.”

He warned that approaches which ignore these responsibilities could damage both the Christian message and public confidence in school-based ministry.

“Evangelistic approaches that disregard these responsibilities risk undermining both the message of the Gospel and the trust placed in Christian ministry within schools.”

The Council also highlighted Fiji’s constitutional protections on freedom of religion, saying they provide the framework for people of different faiths to coexist in mutual respect.

The FCC welcomed the Ministry of Education’s review into the incident and pledged to continue working with the Ministry, member churches and recognised Christian organisations to ensure Christian ministry in schools is conducted responsibly, respectfully and in a manner that reflects the love and character of Jesus Christ.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Minister blames Chaudhry for sugar industry woes
item.description: Minister for Fisheries and Forestry Alitia Bainivalu has accused former prime minister Mahendra Chaudhry of contributing to Fiji’s current sugar industry challenges by encouraging farmers to delay harvesting at the start of the crushing season. In a statement posted on social media, the Minister said the Government had guaranteed a minimum cane price of $85 per tonne to encourage farmers to begin
item.content:encoded:
Minister for Fisheries and Forestry Alitia Bainivalu has accused former prime minister Mahendra Chaudhry of contributing to Fiji’s current sugar industry challenges by encouraging farmers to delay harvesting at the start of the crushing season.

In a statement posted on social media, the Minister said the Government had guaranteed a minimum cane price of $85 per tonne to encourage farmers to begin harvesting on time and ensure the crushing season started as scheduled.

She claimed many farmers responded to the call, recognising that delays would have consequences for the industry.

“Instead, Mahendra Chaudhry urged farmers to hold back their cane while demanding $110 per tonne. That political campaign encouraged delays at a critical time for Fiji’s sugar industry,” she said.

The Minister said the effects of those delays were now being felt across the sector.

“Labour shortages have become more severe, harvesting has fallen behind schedule, concerns over local sugar supplies have grown, and Fiji is now facing the prospect of importing sugar to meet domestic demand.”

She warned that importing sugar could result in higher prices for consumers.

“Imported sugar is unlikely to cost the same as locally produced sugar. If prices rise, it will not be politicians who feel the greatest burden. It will be ordinary Fijian families already struggling with the rising cost of living. It will be parents trying to stretch every dollar. It will be small businesses. It will be those who can least afford another increase in household expenses.”

The Minister said those who encouraged farmers to delay harvesting should accept responsibility for the consequences.

“Those who encouraged farmers to delay harvesting cannot now wash their hands of the consequences. If you tell farmers not to harvest and the crushing season falls behind, you must accept responsibility for the impact your words have had.”

She also urged political leaders not to use the sugar industry for political purposes.

“Our sugar industry should never become a political football. It supports thousands of farming families, sustains rural communities and contributes to our national economy. Leadership means putting farmers and the nation first, not chasing political headlines at the expense of ordinary Fijians.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: ‘There are no premier ministries’: PSC pushes united leadership
item.description: The Public Service Commission has stressed that all Government ministries must work together as equals as it moves to strengthen accountability and performance across the Civil Service. Speaking at a workshop for Permanent Secretaries yesterday, PSC Chair Luke Rokovada said the success of the Government’s public sector reforms would depend on stronger collaboration between ministries.
item.content:encoded:
The Public Service Commission has stressed that all Government ministries must work together as equals as it moves to strengthen accountability and performance across the Civil Service.

Speaking at a workshop for Permanent Secretaries yesterday, PSC Chair Luke Rokovada said the success of the Government’s public sector reforms would depend on stronger collaboration between ministries.

“There are no premiere or special ministries. We are all here to work together in advancing Government’s objectives and delivering better services to our people,” Mr Rokovada said.

The workshop brought together Permanent Secretaries to help develop a new Permanent Secretaries Effectiveness and Development System and a revised Performance Agreement framework, aimed at improving leadership, accountability and service delivery.

The initiative forms part of the wider Functional Review of the Fiji Civil Service announced earlier this year by Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka to modernise Government institutions and strengthen public confidence.

Mr Rokovada said the introduction of Performance Agreements would ensure senior public servants were more closely aligned with the objectives of their ministries while creating a stronger culture of performance.

“Performance Agreements are vital because they improve service delivery and accountability, align leadership responsibilities with the functions of each ministry, identify development needs and provide a sound basis for merit-based advancement,” he said.

He said the Commission remained on track to introduce the new agreements by 30 September 2026, incorporating feedback received during consultations.

Mr Rokovada also announced progress on a new Executive Leadership Programme and the re-establishment of the Senior Executive Service to improve succession planning and leadership capability across Government.

Meanwhile, Permanent Secretary for the Civil Service Pita Tagicakirewa encouraged participants to play an active role in shaping the reforms.

“This workshop is an important opportunity for Permanent Secretaries to contribute meaningfully to the development of two significant leadership documents that will help shape the future of the Civil Service,” Mr Tagicakirewa said.

He urged Permanent Secretaries to provide honest and constructive feedback to ensure the new leadership framework reflects the realities of public sector management before its implementation.

The reforms are expected to strengthen performance, reinforce accountability and foster a more connected leadership culture across the Civil Service, with the ultimate goal of improving services delivered to Fijians.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
